sections.title: Data Storage & Privacy — Insecure Data Storage

items.title: Sensitive data in SharedPreferences / NSUserDefaults
items.description: Tokens, passwords or PII written to SharedPreferences (Android) or NSUserDefaults (iOS) are stored as cleartext XML/plist and are trivially readable on a rooted/jailbroken device or via backup. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Pull /data/data/<pkg>/shared_prefs/*.xml and the app Library/Preferences/*.plist; grep for tokens, passwords, PII. Confirm no secrets are stored here unencrypted. Common tags: mobile, android, ios, storage.

items.title: Unencrypted SQLite / Realm databases
items.description: App databases holding credentials, session data or PII are stored without encryption (no SQLCipher / Realm encryption), exposing all rows on a compromised device. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Extract *.db / *.sqlite / *.realm from the app sandbox and open them; confirm sensitive tables are encrypted, not browsable in plaintext. Common tags: mobile, android, ios, storage.

items.title: Sensitive data on external/shared storage
items.description: Writing sensitive files to external storage (Android getExternalFilesDir/SD card or world-readable paths) exposes them to other apps and removes OS sandbox protection. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Monitor file writes during use; confirm no secrets land on /sdcard or shared storage, and that scoped storage is used on modern Android. Common tags: mobile, android, storage.

Common tags: mobile, android, ios, backup.

Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Confirm secret fields disable copy or clear the clipboard, and that the app does not write sensitive values to UIPasteboard/ClipboardManager. sections.title: Data Storage & Privacy — Keychain / Keystore Usage

items.title: Secrets not stored in Keychain / Keystore
items.description: Cryptographic keys and credentials kept in plain files or preferences instead of the OS secure store lose hardware isolation and OS access controls. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Confirm keys/tokens are created and stored via iOS Keychain Services or Android Keystore rather than custom files or preferences. Common tags: mobile, android, ios, keystore.

items.title: Weak Keychain protection class
items.description: Keychain items using kSecAttrAccessibleAlways or AfterFirstUnlock (with ThisDeviceOnly omitted) remain readable when the device is locked or migrate via backup, weakening protection. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Dump Keychain attributes and confirm sensitive items use WhenUnlockedThisDeviceOnly or WhenPasscodeSetThisDeviceOnly. Common tags: mobile, ios, keychain.

items.title: Hardware-backed keystore / StrongBox not used
items.description: Keys generated in software rather than the TEE/Secure Enclave/StrongBox can be extracted on a rooted device, defeating at-rest key protection. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. items.title: Tapjacking and overlay attacks
items.description: Sensitive Android screens that do not filter touches obscured by overlays can be tapjacked: a malicious app draws over the UI and tricks the user into approving actions or leaking input. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Confirm sensitive views set filterTouchesWhenObscured (or reject MotionEvents with FLAG_WINDOW_IS_OBSCURED) and that the app detects/blocks overlay-based UI redress. On iOS, verify sensitive flows are not capturable via screen recording/overlay where applicable. Common tags: mobile, android, tapjacking, overlay.

items.title: No code obfuscation
items.description: Unobfuscated code with original class/method names makes reverse engineering, logic recovery and bypass development trivial for high-value apps. items.how_to_find: Platform: iOS. Endpoints, encryption keys and tokens placed in res/values, assets, or BuildConfig fields are easily dumped without even decompiling code. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Unpack the APK/IPA and grep res/, assets/, plists and BuildConfig for secret-like values; confirm none are sensitive. Common tags: mobile, android, ios, secrets. Static: decompile/review binary and manifest/plist. Dynamic: instrument with Frida/objection, proxy TLS via Burp. Control: Secrets in strings / resources / assets.
items.how_to_fix: Remediate "Secrets in strings / resources / assets": remove secrets from client, disable exported components, enforce certificate pinning with backup pins, use Android Keystore / iOS Keychain with strict accessibility, disable backups/debug flags in release, and never trust client-side checks alone.

items.title: Secrets hidden in native libraries
items.description: Moving keys/secrets into .so / native code only raises the bar slightly; static analysis of the binary recovers them, so it is not a real protection. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Disassemble bundled native libraries and search for embedded keys/strings; confirm secrets are not merely hidden in native code as the sole defense.
